title: Network Change MTTR Analysis
dataSources: Primary: `index=catalyst sourcetype=cisco:dnac:issue`. from the `issue` modular input in the Cisco Catalyst Add-on (Splunkbase 7538) polling `GET /dna/intent/api/v1/issues`. Default poll interval: 900s (15m). Volume: ~1 event per active issue per poll. RBAC: NETWORK-ADMIN-ROLE. Key fields: `priority` (string), `category` (string), `name` (string), `deviceName` (string), `status` (string), `issueId` (string), `siteId` (string), `lastOccurenceTime` (epoch milliseconds). (issue lifecycle for MTTR calculation). Secondary: `index=catalyst sourcetype=cisco:dnac:audit:logs`. from the `audit_logs` modular input in the Cisco Catalyst Add-on (Splunkbase 7538) polling `GET /dna/data/api/v1/event/event-series/audit-log`. Default poll interval: 300s (5m). Volume: Variable; ~10-100 events/day in typical environments. RBAC: SUPER-ADMIN-ROLE. Key fields: `auditUserName` (string), `auditRequestType` (string), `auditDescription` (string), `auditParentId` (string), `createdDateTime` (epoch milliseconds), `isSystemEvent` (boolean). CIM data model: Change. (configuration change context). Correlation: join on `deviceName` and time window to associate configuration changes with issue resolution.
spl: index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:issue" status="RESOLVED" | eval detect_time=_time | eval resolve_duration_hrs=round((resolved_time-detect_time)/3600,1) | stats avg(resolve_duration_hrs) as avg_mttr_hrs median(resolve_duration_hrs) as median_mttr_hrs p90(resolve_duration_hrs) as p90_mttr_hrs count as resolved_issues by category | join type=left category [search index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:audit:logs" auditRequestType="CONFIG*" | stats count as related_changes by category] | eval change_correlation=if(isnotnull(related_changes),"Changes detected","No changes") | sort avg_mttr_hrs
description: Analyzes mean time to repair (MTTR) for Catalyst Center issues and correlates with configuration changes to determine whether changes are improving or degrading resolution times.
value: MTTR is the key operational efficiency metric. Correlating with configuration changes reveals whether changes are helping (reducing MTTR) or hurting (introducing new issues).
implementation:
1. **Issues:** Requires `cisco:dnac:issue` with `status=RESOLVED`, a valid **creation** time and **resolved** time — the SPL uses `_time` as detect and `resolved_time` as resolution; if your payload uses `creationTime`/`lastUpdatedTime`, rewrite `eval` lines to match **epoch** seconds.
2. **Audit:** `cisco:dnac:audit:logs` from Intent API audit stream; `auditRequestType="CONFIG*"` filters configuration changes. If your field is `auditRequestType` vs `requestType`, adjust.
3. **Join on category:** Both issue and audit must share a stable `category` string; if audit lacks `category`, join on `siteId` + time window instead (advanced).
4. **Schedule:** Weekly or monthly report; store in summary index for trending.
5. **Privacy:** Audit logs may contain usernames — restrict role access to this report.
visualization: Table: category, avg_mttr_hrs, median_mttr_hrs, p90_mttr_hrs, resolved_issues, related_changes, change_correlation; optional timechart of avg MTTR by week; box plot of resolve_duration_hrs.
knownFalsePositives:
**Auto-resolved issues with very short MTTR skewing the average downward.** Transient issues that Assurance auto-resolves within minutes show near-zero MTTR, making the team's response appear faster than it actually is. Distinguish by checking whether `resolve_duration_hrs < 0.1` (under 6 minutes), indicating auto-resolution. Suppress by filtering `| where resolve_duration_hrs >= 0.5` for MTTR analysis to focus on issues that required human intervention.

**Issue resolution timestamp missing or inaccurate for manually closed issues.** When an operator manually closes an issue in Catalyst Center, the `resolved_time` may not accurately reflect when the problem was actually fixed. Distinguish by checking whether `resolved_time` is populated and reasonable. Suppress by filtering `| where isnotnull(resolved_time) AND resolved_time>detect_time`.

**Maintenance-window issues included in MTTR calculation.** Issues generated during planned maintenance have their resolution time determined by the maintenance duration, not the team's operational response. Distinguish by correlating with ITSM change records. Suppress by excluding maintenance-window issues from the MTTR metric.

**Change-related issues where the change itself is the resolution.** Some Assurance issues are created because a configuration change was detected. The change is intentional, and the issue resolves when Assurance re-scans. These are not incidents requiring response. Distinguish by checking whether the `category` is "Configuration" and correlating with audit log configuration changes. Suppress by filtering configuration-change issues from MTTR analysis.

references.title: Catalyst Center Issues API — Cisco DevNet

detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Cisco Catalyst Add-on for Splunk (Splunkbase 7538) with the **issue** modular input enabled to `index=catalyst`, `sourcetype=cisco:dnac:issue`.
• At least 30 days of issue data with both `ACTIVE` and `RESOLVED` status events for meaningful MTTR calculation.
• RBAC: service account with **NETWORK-ADMIN-ROLE**.
• Understanding of MTTR calculation: the time from issue detection (`earliest _time` for an `issueId` with `status=ACTIVE`) to resolution (`latest _time` for the same `issueId` with `status=RESOLVED`).

Step 1 — Configure data collection
The TA polls `GET /dna/intent/api/v1/issue` at the configured interval (default 900 seconds). Each poll returns all currently active issues. Resolved issues are captured when their `status` changes from ACTIVE to RESOLVED.

Key fields for MTTR analysis:
• `issueId`: unique issue identifier.
• `status`: "ACTIVE" or "RESOLVED".
• `priority`: P1-P4 severity.
• `name`: issue type name.
• `deviceName`: affected device.
• `category`: issue category.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert

```spl
index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:issue"
| stats earliest(_time) as detect_time latest(_time) as last_seen latest(status) as final_status by issueId, name, priority, deviceName, category
| where final_status="RESOLVED"
| eval resolve_duration_hrs=round((last_seen-detect_time)/3600,2)
| stats avg(resolve_duration_hrs) as avg_mttr_hrs median(resolve_duration_hrs) as median_mttr_hrs p95(resolve_duration_hrs) as p95_mttr_hrs count as resolved_count by priority
| eval avg_mttr_display=round(avg_mttr_hrs,1)." hrs"
| sort priority
```

Understanding this SPL:
• **`earliest(_time) as detect_time`**: First time the issue appeared in the data — this is when Catalyst Center detected the issue.
• **`latest(_time) as last_seen` with `final_status="RESOLVED"`**: The last event for the issue when it was resolved. The delta between detection and resolution is the MTTR.
• **`where final_status="RESOLVED"`**: Only includes issues that have been resolved — active issues cannot have an MTTR.
• **`avg`, `median`, `p95`**: Multiple MTTR metrics. Median is more robust than average (not skewed by outliers). P95 shows the worst-case resolution time.
• **`by priority`**: Separates MTTR by priority level. P1 issues should have the shortest MTTR; P4 issues may have longer resolution times.

Step 3 — Validate
• **Manual verification:** Pick 3-5 resolved issues from the Splunk results. In Catalyst Center > Assurance > Issues, find the same issue and verify the detection and resolution timestamps match.
• **Auto-resolved exclusion:** Check whether `resolve_duration_hrs < 0.1` (under 6 minutes) issues are included. These are typically auto-resolved by Assurance and should be excluded from MTTR analysis for operational performance measurement.

Step 4 — Operationalize
• **Dashboard:** MTTR summary tiles by priority (P1, P2, P3, P4), MTTR trend timechart over 30 days (show weekly averages), and a table of the slowest-resolved issues for process improvement.
• **SLA tracking:** Compare MTTR against SLA targets (e.g., P1: <1 hour, P2: <4 hours, P3: <24 hours). Highlight SLA breaches in red.
• **Trend analysis:** Track MTTR week-over-week to measure operational improvement.

Step 5 — Troubleshoot
• **MTTR appears artificially short:** Auto-resolved issues (Assurance resolving transient issues within minutes) skew the average down. Filter with `| where resolve_duration_hrs >= 0.5` for human-response MTTR.
• **MTTR appears artificially long:** Issues with incorrect or missing resolution timestamps inflate MTTR. Filter `| where isnotnull(last_seen) AND last_seen > detect_time`.
• **Maintenance-window issues inflating MTTR:** Issues created during maintenance have MTTR determined by the maintenance duration. Exclude using a `catalyst_maintenance_windows` lookup.
• **Not enough resolved issues for analysis:** Extend the time window to 90 days or longer. If few issues are resolved, the environment may be too small for statistical MTTR analysis.
• **No issue events arriving:** verify the `issue` input is enabled and check `index=_internal` for API error or endpoint timeout messages from the TA.
• **Missing fields or empty values:** some issue fields vary by Catalyst Center version; run `| fieldsummary` to identify NULL or zero values that need `fillnull`.
• **Fewer events than the Catalyst Center UI shows:** the API returns only active issues by default; if credential access is denied, check the service account RBAC role.
grandmaExplanation: We measure how long it takes to fix network problems and whether recent changes are making things better or worse.
